module.label: Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen

use_when: Infant, child, or adolescent with abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, suspected appendicitis, gastroenteritis, UTI-related abdominal symptoms, or possible acute abdomen.

Pediatric abdominal or vomiting workup needs baseline safety data, age-band routing, dehydration assessment, surgical red flags, pregnancy-capable adolescent routing, and source-backed imaging/testing choices.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use adult abdominal pain, pelvic pain, GU, renal, or infection workup as appropriate.
Unstable child with shock, peritonitis, bilious vomiting, suspected obstruction, testicular torsion, or possible ectopic pregnancy; escalate immediately while documenting the focused workup.
Primary fever or suspected sepsis without abdominal, vomiting, diarrheal, urinary, pelvic, or scrotal symptoms; use pediatric fever, infection, or sepsis pathway.
Pregnant or recently postpartum adolescent with systemic illness or obstetric concern; add obstetric emergency and pregnancy-specific escalation guidance.

differentialBuckets.label: Appendicitis or surgical acute abdomen with localized or progressive abdominal pain, peritoneal signs, obstruction features, or persistent focal tenderness
differentialBuckets.action: Use serial abdominal exams, pediatric surgical escalation when concern persists, and ultrasound-first appendicitis imaging for children and adolescents when imaging is needed.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Appendicitis and other surgical abdominal emergencies can be missed when pediatric pain is treated as generic gastroenteritis or adult abdominal pain.

source.source_section: diagnostic imaging of suspected acute appendicitis in children and adolescents
source.implementation_notes: Pair imaging choice with bedside severity, local pediatric surgery access, and shared decision-making after equivocal ultrasound.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pediatric appendicitis, bowel obstruction, intussusception, incarcerated hernia, perforation, or other surgical acute abdomen.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes need for urgent surgical consultation, serial examination, ultrasound-first imaging, MRI or CT after equivocal ultrasound, NPO status, IV access, analgesia, and transfer planning.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this broad surgical-frame row; use source-backed appendicitis imaging evidence and bedside red flags.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not reassure from a single benign exam when symptoms progress; young children can present atypically and may need repeated examination.

differentialBuckets.label: Gastroenteritis or dehydration with vomiting, diarrhea, intake decline, reduced urine output, or exposure/travel clues
differentialBuckets.action: Separate likely gastroenteritis from alternative diagnoses, document dehydration risk, and use oral rehydration or IV/enteral fluids according to severity.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Children with vomiting or diarrhea need dehydration assessment and explicit alternative-diagnosis screening so serious abdominal, neurologic, or septic illness is not missed.

source.source_section: assessment, alternative diagnoses, dehydration risk, stool microbiology, oral rehydration, and home advice
source.implementation_notes: NICE CG84 is scoped to children under 5; for older children use the same dehydration principles alongside local pediatric gastroenteritis pathways.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Likely infectious gastroenteritis with or without dehydration, while screening for alternative diagnoses.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes ORS plan, feeding advice, stool testing, public health/outbreak steps, IV fluid need, reassessment, and discharge safety-netting.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for this broad symptom frame; use guideline dehydration and alternative-diagnosis criteria.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not use gastroenteritis framing alone when diarrhea is absent, vomiting is bilious, pain is severe/localized, mental status changes, or sepsis features are present.

differentialBuckets.label: UTI, pyelonephritis, or urinary source with dysuria, frequency, malodorous urine, hematuria, flank pain, suprapubic tenderness, fever, or prior UTI
differentialBuckets.action: Ask urinary symptoms atomically and obtain urine testing before antibiotics when feasible, without delaying urgent treatment in high-risk or septic children.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Pediatric UTI can present with abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, or nonlocalizing symptoms; missing it can delay antibiotics and renal-risk follow-up.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs increasing UTI likelihood, urine collection, microscopy/culture, referral, and treatment principles
source.implementation_notes: Use age-appropriate urine collection and local pediatric antimicrobial guidance; do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pediatric UTI, pyelonephritis, or urinary source of abdominal pain, vomiting, or fever.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes urinalysis/culture, antibiotics, renal imaging/follow-up eligibility, constipation/dysfunctional elimination management, and sepsis escalation.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level likelihood ratios are not supplied by this module; NICE symptom clusters and urine testing guide workup.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Urinary symptoms may be absent in younger children; persistent symptoms after a negative urine screen require reassessment for abdominal, sepsis, or other sources.

differentialBuckets.label: Pregnancy-related emergency or ectopic pregnancy in a pregnancy-capable adolescent with lower abdominal or pelvic pain, bleeding, syncope, shoulder-tip pain, or GI symptoms
differentialBuckets.action: For postmenarchal or pregnancy-capable adolescents, confirm pregnancy possibility and obtain pregnancy testing before attributing lower abdominal or pelvic pain to GI causes.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Ectopic pregnancy can present with abdominal pain, GI symptoms, urinary symptoms, dizziness, syncope, shoulder-tip pain, or vaginal bleeding and changes urgency immediately.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs of ectopic pregnancy and need for further investigation when symptoms persist
source.implementation_notes: Use confidential adolescent care practices and local pregnancy-of-unknown-location or ectopic pathway when pregnancy is possible.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Ectopic pregnancy, pregnancy of unknown location, miscarriage, or pregnancy-related abdominal or pelvic emergency.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes pregnancy test, pelvic/obstetric pathway, ultrasound/beta-hCG interpretation, urgent gynecology or ED escalation, and confidentiality-sensitive documentation.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not attached to this safety-frame row; pregnancy testing and symptom-specific guideline criteria drive management.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not exclude pregnancy solely from reported sexual history, age assumption, or absence of missed period if pregnancy is biologically possible.

differentialBuckets.label: Testicular torsion or acute scrotum presenting with abdominal pain, vomiting, testicular pain, swelling, high-riding testis, or absent cremasteric reflex
differentialBuckets.action: Ask and examine for scrotal/testicular symptoms when male anatomy is possible; escalate urgently when torsion is possible rather than waiting for a complete abdominal workup.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Torsion can present as abdominal pain or vomiting and is time-sensitive; delayed recognition can lead to testicular loss.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including lower abdominal, pelvic, or testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: This module prompts recognition and urgent routing; use the dedicated acute scrotum pathway for definitive evaluation.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Testicular torsion or acute scrotal pathology presenting with abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes urgent urology/surgery consultation, scrotal exam, Doppler ultrasound only if it does not delay intervention, and transfer urgency.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned in this pediatric abdominal frame; use the dedicated acute scrotum workup when triggered.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Absence of reported testicular pain does not reliably exclude torsion in younger children; ask directly when anatomy is possible.

safetyChecks.label: Measure blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure blood pressure with an age-appropriate cuff and document perfusion context, pain, and repeat value if abnormal.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypotension or abnormal perfusion changes concern for shock, dehydration, sepsis, hemorrhage, or ruptured ectopic pregnancy in an adolescent.

source.source_section: age-adjusted physiologic risk features and shock recognition in children and young people
source.implementation_notes: Use local age-specific thresholds; do not apply adult hypotension cutoffs alone.

safetyChecks.management_change: Low blood pressure or concerning perfusion changes fluid resuscitation, sepsis/surgical escalation, transfer, and monitoring intensity.
safetyChecks.limitations: Pain, anxiety, wrong cuff size, and movement can distort readings; reassess abnormal values.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to blood pressure as baseline safety data; management is threshold- and trajectory-driven.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate
safetyChecks.action: Measure heart rate and interpret with age, pain, fever, dehydration, perfusion, and trend.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachycardia can signal pain, dehydration, sepsis, hemorrhage, shock, or worsening surgical abdomen.

source.source_section: age-adjusted heart-rate risk features for suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Combine with blood pressure, capillary refill, mental status, urine output, and pain control response.

safetyChecks.management_change: Persistent tachycardia changes dehydration treatment, analgesia reassessment, sepsis screening, surgical escalation, and disposition.
safetyChecks.limitations: Fever, anxiety, pain, medications, and crying affect rate; trend after analgesia or fluids when feasible.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to heart rate as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate
safetyChecks.action: Count respiratory rate for a full minute when feasible and compare with age-adjusted thresholds.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachypnea can signal sepsis, dehydration, pain, metabolic acidosis, pneumonia mimic, or respiratory compromise in vomiting illness.

source.source_section: age-adjusted respiratory risk features for suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Interpret with oxygen saturation, work of breathing, fever, pain, and bedside glucose/ketone concern.

safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal respiratory rate changes sepsis/DKA mimic screening, oxygen/respiratory evaluation, monitoring, and level of care.
safetyChecks.limitations: Crying and pain may elevate respiratory rate; reassess after comfort measures when safe.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.action: Measure temperature and document method, antipyretic timing, and fever trajectory.
safetyChecks.rationale: Fever changes concern for appendicitis complications, UTI/pyelonephritis, gastroenteritis, sepsis, pneumonia mimic, and alternative diagnoses.

source.source_section: alternative diagnoses when fever accompanies vomiting or diarrhea
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric fever/sepsis pathway when fever plus physiologic risk features or unclear source is present.

safetyChecks.management_change: Fever changes source localization, urine testing, stool testing, imaging/antibiotics consideration, sepsis escalation, and safety-netting.
safetyChecks.limitations: Temperature alone does not diagnose appendicitis, UTI, gastroenteritis, or sepsis; combine with trajectory and exam.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure current weight
safetyChecks.action: Measure or verify current weight in kilograms before fluid, antiemetic, analgesic, antibiotic, or transfer dosing decisions.
safetyChecks.rationale: Pediatric abdominal and vomiting care often uses weight-based fluids and medications; missing weight can cause dosing errors.

source.source_section: oral rehydration and fluid replacement by weight
source.implementation_notes: Use measured weight when feasible; emergency care may use institutional weight-estimation tools if treatment cannot wait.

safetyChecks.management_change: Weight changes ORS volume, IV fluid planning, medication dosing, antibiotic selection logistics, and transfer handoff.
safetyChecks.limitations: Do not delay emergency treatment solely to obtain a measured weight in an unstable child.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to weight as medication-safety and fluid-planning data.

safetyChecks.label: Document mental status
safetyChecks.action: Document alertness, irritability, lethargy, responsiveness to parent/caregiver, and change from baseline.
safetyChecks.rationale: Altered responsiveness changes concern for shock, sepsis, dehydration, hypoglycemia, DKA mimic, meningitis, or intracranial cause of vomiting.

source.source_section: dehydration, shock, and alternative diagnoses including altered consciousness
source.implementation_notes: Pair with bedside glucose and sepsis screening when mental status is abnormal.

safetyChecks.management_change: Lethargy or altered consciousness changes urgent glucose check, fluids, sepsis pathway, neurologic evaluation, monitoring, and disposition.
safetyChecks.limitations: Young age, sleep, pain, fear, and medications can complicate interpretation; use caregiver baseline and repeat assessments.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to mental status as baseline acuity data.

safetyChecks.label: Document intake and urine output
safetyChecks.action: Document usual intake, last successful oral intake, vomiting frequency, diarrhea frequency, wet nappies or urine output, and last urine.
safetyChecks.rationale: Intake and urine output change dehydration severity, oral versus IV/enteral fluids, admission, and safety-netting.
findings_options: fewer wet nappies or reduced urine
unable to obtain intake or urine history

source.source_section: dehydration risk, reduced urine output, stool/vomit counts, rehydration, and home advice
source.implementation_notes: Make this a documented baseline item rather than a physical exam maneuver.

safetyChecks.management_change: Poor intake, persistent vomiting, frequent stools, or reduced urine output changes rehydration route, reassessment timing, admission, and caregiver instructions.
safetyChecks.limitations: Caregiver estimates may be imprecise; trend and objective urine/stool counts when possible.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to intake/output as baseline safety data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure bedside glucose
safetyChecks.action: Measure point-of-care glucose when the child is unwell, altered, dehydrated, has persistent vomiting without diarrhea, polyuria/polydipsia, weight loss, diabetes, or acidosis concern.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic crisis can present with vomiting or abdominal pain and change immediate management.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis and need to consider serious non-gastroenteritis causes
source.implementation_notes: If glucose is abnormal, route to the hyperglycemia/DKA or hypoglycemia local pathway rather than treating as isolated GI illness.

safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal bedside glucose changes immediate dextrose/insulin/ketone/acidosis workup, fluid plan, monitoring, and level of care.
safetyChecks.limitations: A normal glucose does not exclude dehydration, sepsis, surgical abdomen, or intracranial causes of vomiting.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to bedside glucose as baseline safety data.

requiredQuestions.label: Pediatric abdominal age band
requiredQuestions.text: Which age band applies: under 6 weeks, 6 weeks to under 3 months, 3 to 11 months, 12 to 23 months, 2 to 4 years, 5 to 11 years, or 12 to 15 years?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Age changes appendicitis presentation, dehydration risk, urine testing approach, pregnancy applicability, and neonatal/infant escalation.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Age band changes differential, imaging strategy, urine collection route, ORS risk, febrile infant routing, transfer threshold, and adolescent confidential history.

source.source_section: acute abdominal pain assessment and age-sensitive differential diagnosis
source.implementation_notes: Use exact age to prevent adult pathway leakage and to decide adolescent pregnancy/scrotal/gynecologic routing.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to age-band eligibility; it determines pathway scope and risk interpretation.

requiredQuestions.label: Pain location and trajectory
requiredQuestions.text: Where is the pain now, where did it start, has it migrated, and is it becoming localized, constant, severe, or worse with movement?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every child with abdominal pain or vomiting where pain may be present but under-described.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies appendicitis, peritonitis, obstruction, pelvic pathology, renal/urinary source, and need for repeated exam.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Localized, migrating, progressive, or movement-related pain changes surgical consultation, imaging, analgesia, observation, and discharge safety.

source.source_section: history features, repeated examination, and investigation guided by likely cause
source.implementation_notes: Document denied and endorsed pain features separately; do not bundle into generic abdominal history.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available here; use trajectory and localization to trigger source-backed surgical and imaging pathways.

requiredQuestions.label: Vomiting character
requiredQuestions.text: Is vomiting bilious green, bloody, persistent without diarrhea, projectile, early-morning with headache, or associated with abdominal distension?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every vomiting child and when abdominal pain is accompanied by nausea, poor intake, or dehydration.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates gastroenteritis from obstruction, intracranial cause, sepsis, surgical abdomen, and other dangerous mimics.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Bilious, bloody, persistent, distension-associated, or neurologic-pattern vomiting changes emergency referral, imaging, fluids, observation, and specialty consultation.

source.source_section: vomiting key points and serious alternative diagnoses
source.implementation_notes: Bilious green vomiting should be treated as obstruction until proven otherwise.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this safety screen; guideline red flags determine escalation.

requiredQuestions.label: Diarrhea stool and exposure features
requiredQuestions.text: Are there loose or watery stools, blood or mucus in stool, travel, outbreak exposure, sick contacts, food exposure, or no improvement by day 7?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when vomiting, diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain, or dehydration could represent gastroenteritis or enteric infection.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes likely gastroenteritis from alternative diagnoses and identifies stool microbiology or public-health triggers.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Blood/mucus, travel, outbreak, immunocompromise, suspected septicemia, or prolonged illness changes stool testing, isolation/public health, antibiotics consideration, and follow-up.

source.source_section: diagnosis of gastroenteritis and stool microbiology indications
source.implementation_notes: Use multi-select documentation because more than one exposure or stool feature can be true.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this stool and exposure item; positive features trigger guideline-based testing and public-health steps.

requiredQuestions.label: Dehydration risk history
requiredQuestions.text: Has the child had reduced intake, fewer wet nappies or less urine, more than 5 diarrheal stools in 24 hours, more than 2 vomits in 24 hours, inability to keep fluids down, age under 1 year, or low birth weight?
options: more than 5 diarrheal stools in 24 hours
age under 1 year or low birth weight
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when vomiting, diarrhea, poor intake, fever, or abdominal pain could cause dehydration.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies dehydration risk and shock trajectory before discharge or oral rehydration planning.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive risk factors change ORS use, observation, rehydration route, reassessment timing, admission threshold, and home safety-netting.

source.source_section: risk factors for dehydration and clinical dehydration/shock assessment
source.implementation_notes: This is explicitly multi-select; each endorsed risk changes hydration and disposition.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this combined dehydration risk item; use guideline risk factors and bedside hydration findings.

requiredQuestions.label: Urinary symptom cluster
requiredQuestions.text: Are dysuria, urinary frequency, new bedwetting, malodorous urine, dark or cloudy urine, visible hematuria, flank pain, suprapubic pain, fever or shivering, prior UTI, or reduced intake present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, nonspecific illness, or urinary concern in children and young people.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for UTI or pyelonephritis symptoms that can present as abdominal pain, vomiting, or fever.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Endorsed urinary symptoms change urinalysis/culture, antibiotics, referral, follow-up, and constipation/dysfunctional elimination management.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs that increase likelihood of UTI in babies, children, and young people
source.implementation_notes: Use multi-select positive/negative documentation; urinary features should not be hidden inside generic abdominal history.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this urinary symptom cluster; NICE symptom groups and urine testing guide management.

requiredQuestions.label: Adolescent pregnancy context
requiredQuestions.text: For a postmenarchal or pregnancy-capable adolescent, is pregnancy possible, is there missed or late period, vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, syncope, shoulder-tip pain, rectal pressure, or prior ectopic pregnancy?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially when the patient is postmenarchal, pregnancy-capable, has lower abdominal/pelvic pain, vomiting, syncope, bleeding, or pregnancy possibility.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies pregnancy or ectopic pregnancy risk before attributing adolescent lower abdominal symptoms to GI, UTI, or menstrual causes.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Pregnancy possibility changes pregnancy test, ultrasound/beta-hCG pathway, urgent gynecology/ED escalation, confidentiality, and documentation.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs of ectopic pregnancy and pregnancy-related abdominal or pelvic pain
source.implementation_notes: Do not remove this from adolescent workflows unless a separate reproductive applicability gate supplies equivalent safety coverage.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to pregnancy possibility screening; pregnancy testing and ectopic-symptom pathways are required when pregnancy is possible.

requiredQuestions.label: Scrotal or testicular symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: If male anatomy is possible, is there testicular pain, scrotal swelling, high-riding testis, nausea with scrotal pain, trauma, or pain that started suddenly?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when male anatomy is possible, abdominal pain is lower abdominal, vomiting is prominent, or the child is too young to localize pain reliably.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for testicular torsion or acute scrotum presenting as abdominal pain or vomiting.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive symptoms change urgent scrotal exam, urology/surgical escalation, imaging only if it does not delay care, and transfer planning.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including lower abdominal, pelvic, or testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: Route to the dedicated acute scrotum workup when positive.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this screening row; time-sensitive torsion concern changes action regardless.

conditionalQuestions.label: Appendicitis risk features
conditionalQuestions.text: Are right lower quadrant pain, pain migration, anorexia, fever, pain with cough, pain with hopping, worsening focal tenderness, or prior equivocal ultrasound present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when appendicitis, right lower quadrant pain, focal tenderness, migration, or equivocal imaging is part of the presentation.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Supports pediatric appendicitis risk stratification while preventing risk scores from becoming a standalone rule-out.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change ultrasound-first imaging, serial exam, surgical consultation, observation, and MRI/CT after equivocal ultrasound.

source.source_section: pediatric appendicitis clinical prediction rules and imaging recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Clinical prediction rules may risk-stratify but should not be the sole basis for no advanced imaging in pediatric suspected appendicitis.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this symptom cluster; ACEP summarizes likelihood ratios for imaging strategies, and symptoms alone are not sufficient to rule out appendicitis.

conditionalQuestions.label: Obstruction or intussusception features
conditionalQuestions.text: Are bilious green vomiting, abdominal distension, intermittent severe colicky pain, drawing legs up, pallor or lethargy during episodes, bloody stool, or currant-jelly stool present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when vomiting is bilious, pain is episodic/severe, distension is present, stool is bloody, or obstruction/intussusception is possible.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for bowel obstruction, intussusception, malrotation/volvulus, or other surgical emergency.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change urgent surgical/ED escalation, abdominal imaging, NPO/IV access, analgesia, and transfer.

source.source_section: bilious vomiting and serious causes of vomiting
source.implementation_notes: Bilious vomiting is obstruction until proven otherwise; do not wait for broad workup completion.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this red-flag cluster; guideline action is escalation rather than probabilistic reassurance.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ectopic pregnancy warning features
conditionalQuestions.text: If pregnancy is possible, are pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding, dizziness, fainting or syncope, shoulder-tip pain, rectal pressure, urinary symptoms, or passage of tissue present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially when pregnancy is possible or adolescent lower abdominal/pelvic symptoms are present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies ectopic pregnancy or pregnancy-related emergency symptoms that overlap with GI/GU complaints.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change pregnancy test urgency, pelvic ultrasound/beta-hCG pathway, gynecology/ED escalation, and transfer.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs of ectopic pregnancy
source.implementation_notes: Symptoms can include GI and urinary complaints; maintain pregnancy-safe routing even when the initial complaint is vomiting.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this symptom list; pregnancy testing and ectopic pathway thresholds change management.

conditionalQuestions.label: Stool microbiology triggers
conditionalQuestions.text: Are blood or mucus in stool, suspected septicemia, immunocompromise, recent foreign travel, no improvement by day 7, diagnostic uncertainty, or outbreak/public-health concern present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when diarrhea is present or enteric infection testing is being considered.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies when stool microbiology or public-health steps are guideline-supported.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive triggers change stool testing, isolation/public-health notification, antimicrobial consideration, and follow-up.

source.source_section: indications for stool microbiology and public health notification
source.implementation_notes: Avoid routine stool testing when none of these triggers are present.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this testing-trigger item; guideline indications determine testing.

conditionalQuestions.label: Testicular torsion warning features
conditionalQuestions.text: If male anatomy is possible, is there sudden testicular pain, scrotal swelling, high-riding testis, nausea/vomiting with scrotal pain, or absent cremasteric reflex reported or observed?
options: nausea or vomiting with scrotal pain
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when testicular/scrotal symptoms are present, lower abdominal pain is unexplained, or vomiting is prominent in a child with male anatomy.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies time-sensitive acute scrotum that can masquerade as abdominal pain.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change urgent urology/surgical consultation and imaging timing; do not delay intervention for routine abdominal workup.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: This is a trigger to use the acute scrotum validated intent or local torsion pathway.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this warning-feature screen; torsion is time-sensitive and management-changing.

requiredExam.label: Inspect abdomen
requiredExam.technique: Observe abdominal contour, distension, visible peristalsis, scars, bruising, hernia bulges, and the child's spontaneous movement before palpation.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every pediatric abdominal pain or vomiting workup before palpation.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Distension, obstruction, trauma, hernia, peritonitis behavior, or visible surgical clue.

source.source_section: clinical assessment and examination of acute abdominal pain
source.implementation_notes: Keep inspection separate from vitals and palpation.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for this inspection item; findings trigger obstruction, trauma, hernia, or peritonitis pathways.
requiredExam.management_change: Distension, visible peristalsis, bruising, or hernia changes imaging, surgical consultation, analgesia, NPO/IV access, and transfer urgency.
requiredExam.limitations: A normal inspection does not exclude appendicitis, UTI, ectopic pregnancy, or early obstruction.

requiredExam.label: Auscultate bowel sounds
requiredExam.technique: Auscultate the abdomen before deep palpation and document bowel sounds as normal, high-pitched/tinkling, absent, or limited by crying/noise.
findings_options: limited by crying or ambient noise
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when vomiting, distension, obstruction, ileus, or surgical abdomen is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Obstruction, ileus, severe gastroenteritis, or surgical abdomen context.

source.source_section: clinical assessment of acute abdominal pain and investigation guided by likely cause
source.implementation_notes: Document as one bedside maneuver, not as part of a generic focused exam bundle.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for bowel sounds in this module; abnormal findings support but do not prove obstruction or ileus.
requiredExam.management_change: High-pitched or absent sounds with symptoms change obstruction/ileus concern, imaging, observation, and surgical escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Bowel sounds are variable and should not rule out appendicitis or obstruction when other red flags are present.

requiredExam.label: Palpate abdomen for focal tenderness
requiredExam.technique: Palpate gently away from the painful area first, then localize tenderness by quadrant while watching the child's face and guarding response.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every abdominal pain workup after initial observation and analgesia when feasible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Appendicitis, cholecystitis, UTI/suprapubic source, constipation, peritonitis, or other localized pathology.

source.source_section: acute abdominal pain examination and repeated examination
source.implementation_notes: Appropriate analgesia should be provided; repeated exam is useful when diagnosis is uncertain.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for this broad palpation item; focal tenderness changes imaging and surgical/urinary pathways.
requiredExam.management_change: Focal tenderness changes ultrasound-first appendicitis imaging, urine testing, surgical consult, observation, and discharge safety.
requiredExam.limitations: Young children may guard or cry nonspecifically; repeat after analgesia and caregiver comfort when feasible.

requiredExam.label: Test rebound tenderness
requiredExam.technique: If tolerated and clinically appropriate, gently assess for rebound or release tenderness; avoid repeated painful testing when peritonitis is already clinically evident.
findings_options: not performed because escalation already indicated
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when peritoneal irritation is suspected and the child can tolerate the maneuver.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Peritonitis, perforation, appendicitis complication, or other surgical abdomen.

source.source_section: red flags, peritonism, and acute abdominal pain examination
source.implementation_notes: Escalate on peritonism; do not over-test if pain or distress makes the maneuver harmful.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available here; rebound is a peritoneal danger sign and should be interpreted with the full exam.
requiredExam.management_change: Rebound tenderness changes urgent surgical/ED escalation, imaging, NPO/IV access, antibiotics consideration, and transfer.
requiredExam.limitations: Absence of rebound does not exclude appendicitis; pain, age, and distress affect reliability.

requiredExam.label: Test capillary refill
requiredExam.technique: Press a warm finger or sternum site for about 5 seconds and document refill time, skin temperature, color, and perfusion context.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when vomiting, diarrhea, fever, poor intake, dehydration, or shock is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Clinical dehydration, shock, sepsis physiology, or poor perfusion.

source.source_section: clinical dehydration and shock features including capillary refill and extremity perfusion
source.implementation_notes: Capillary refill is a bedside perfusion maneuver; blood pressure and heart rate remain baseline safety data.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; use guideline dehydration/shock features and trend.
requiredExam.management_change: Delayed refill or cold/mottled extremities changes rehydration route, sepsis/surgical escalation, monitoring, and disposition.
requiredExam.limitations: Ambient temperature, lighting, skin pigmentation, and site selection affect interpretation; use with mental status and urine output.

conditionalExam.label: Percuss abdomen for peritoneal irritation
conditionalExam.technique: Gently percuss each quadrant or use cough/hop observation in an age-appropriate way to assess pain from peritoneal irritation.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when localized, movement-related, or peritoneal pain is suspected and the child can tolerate it.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Peritoneal irritation from appendicitis, perforation, or other surgical abdomen.

source.source_section: acute abdominal pain assessment and surgical red flags
source.implementation_notes: Use age-appropriate maneuver and avoid repeated painful testing.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for this conditional maneuver; positive findings change escalation.
conditionalExam.management_change: Pain with percussion, cough, or hop changes surgical consultation, ultrasound-first imaging, observation, and discharge safety.
conditionalExam.limitations: Negative testing does not rule out appendicitis; reliability depends on age, pain, and cooperation.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect scrotum
conditionalExam.technique: With consent and privacy, inspect for swelling, erythema, asymmetry, high-riding testis, trauma, or visible hernia when male anatomy is possible.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when scrotal/testicular symptoms are present, male anatomy is possible, or lower abdominal pain/vomiting is unexplained.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Testicular torsion, acute scrotum, incarcerated hernia, trauma, or referred abdominal pain source.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including lower abdominal, pelvic, or testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: If torsion is possible, route urgently to acute scrotum pathway.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in this module; time-sensitive torsion concern should drive urgent action.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abnormal inspection changes urgent urology/surgical escalation and avoids delayed abdominal-only workup.
conditionalExam.limitations: A normal inspection does not exclude torsion; use dedicated acute scrotum workup if symptoms persist.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate testis position
conditionalExam.technique: With consent and privacy, gently palpate testis lie and position, comparing sides when possible and stopping if pain is severe.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when torsion or acute scrotum is possible and examination will not delay urgent care.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Testicular torsion, acute scrotal pathology, or hernia-related pain.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: Avoid false reassurance; escalate when torsion remains possible.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available here; use dedicated acute scrotum pathway for evidence-specific exam interpretation.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abnormal lie or high-riding position changes urgent urology/surgical consultation and transfer urgency.
conditionalExam.limitations: Pain, age, and consent can limit exam; do not delay escalation for a perfect exam.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect oral mucosa
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect lips, tongue, and oral mucosa for moisture, tears when crying, and ability to tolerate oral fluids.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when dehydration, poor intake, vomiting, diarrhea, or reduced urine output is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Clinical dehydration and oral rehydration feasibility.

source.source_section: clinical dehydration and rehydration assessment
source.implementation_notes: Use with intake/output, capillary refill, mental status, and weight.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available; dehydration findings change rehydration and disposition.
conditionalExam.management_change: Dry mucosa, absent tears, or inability to drink changes ORS supervision, NG/IV fluids, observation, and admission threshold.
conditionalExam.limitations: Mucosa can be misleading after drinking or mouth breathing; interpret with urine output and perfusion.

redFlags.label: Bilious green vomiting or obstruction concern
redFlags.action: Treat bilious green vomiting, progressive distension, severe colicky pain, or obstruction concern as urgent surgical/ED escalation.
redFlags.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a child can indicate obstruction until proven otherwise and should not be managed as simple gastroenteritis.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Bowel obstruction, malrotation/volvulus, intussusception, or other surgical emergency.

source.source_section: key points for bilious vomiting and urgent surgical referral
source.implementation_notes: Escalate while arranging imaging and transfer; do not wait for routine outpatient testing.

redFlags.management_change: Changes to urgent surgical referral, NPO, IV access, fluids, analgesia, imaging, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this danger trigger; guideline action is urgent evaluation.
redFlags.limitations: Color descriptions can be unreliable; clarify green bile versus yellow gastric contents when possible without delaying escalation.

redFlags.label: Peritonitis or progressive localized pain
redFlags.action: Escalate for urgent surgical/ED evaluation when guarding, rebound, pain with movement, progressive localized tenderness, distension, or toxic appearance is present.
redFlags.rationale: Peritonitis or worsening focal pain can indicate appendicitis complication, perforation, obstruction, or another surgical abdomen.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Peritonitis, perforated appendicitis, bowel obstruction, incarcerated hernia, or other acute abdomen.

source.source_section: red flags, repeated examination, and acute abdominal pain management
source.implementation_notes: Provide appropriate analgesia; pain relief should not be treated as masking serious disease.

redFlags.management_change: Changes surgical consultation, ultrasound/advanced imaging, NPO/IV access, antibiotics consideration, and observation or transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is unavailable for this broad red flag; bedside findings drive urgent management.
redFlags.limitations: Young children may not show classic peritoneal signs; repeated exam and caregiver report matter.

redFlags.label: Shock dehydration or altered mental status
redFlags.action: Escalate immediately when altered responsiveness, cold or mottled extremities, capillary refill over 3 seconds, hypotension, no urine, inability to tolerate fluids, or severe dehydration is present.
redFlags.rationale: Shock or severe dehydration changes immediate fluids, monitoring, sepsis screening, and disposition.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Hypovolemic shock, septic shock, severe dehydration, metabolic crisis, or high-acuity abdominal illness.

source.source_section: clinical dehydration, shock, reduced urine output, and rehydration
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric sepsis pathway if infection or physiologic risk features coexist.

redFlags.management_change: Changes to supervised ORS, NG/IV fluids, bolus/resuscitation pathway, sepsis screening, admission, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to dehydration/shock danger signs; management is threshold- and physiology-driven.
redFlags.limitations: Older children may compensate until late; trend mental status, perfusion, urine output, and vitals.

redFlags.label: Possible ectopic pregnancy emergency
redFlags.action: Escalate urgently when pregnancy is possible and abdominal/pelvic pain is accompanied by bleeding, syncope, dizziness, shoulder-tip pain, rectal pressure, pallor, or peritoneal signs.
redFlags.rationale: Ectopic pregnancy can rupture and present with GI or urinary symptoms, making adolescent lower abdominal pain unsafe to treat as generic GI illness.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Ectopic pregnancy, ruptured ectopic pregnancy, pregnancy of unknown location, or miscarriage complication.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs of ectopic pregnancy
source.implementation_notes: Use confidentiality-sensitive adolescent care and local obstetric/gynecology emergency pathway.

redFlags.management_change: Changes pregnancy testing, urgent ultrasound/beta-hCG pathway, gynecology/ED consultation, resuscitation, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to this emergency trigger; pregnancy testing and symptoms determine escalation.
redFlags.limitations: Do not exclude pregnancy by assumption; obtain a confidential history and test when biologically possible.

redFlags.label: Possible testicular torsion emergency
redFlags.action: Escalate urgently when sudden testicular pain, scrotal swelling, high-riding testis, severe scrotal tenderness, or vomiting with scrotal symptoms is present.
redFlags.rationale: Torsion is time-sensitive and can present as abdominal pain or vomiting; waiting for a routine abdominal workup risks testicular loss.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: Route to acute scrotum workup or local emergency urology process.

redFlags.management_change: Changes urgent urology/surgical consultation, transfer, and whether imaging is allowed to delay intervention.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned here; time-sensitive emergency pathway takes priority.
redFlags.limitations: Symptoms may be poorly localized in children; ask directly and examine when appropriate.

initialTests.label: Right lower quadrant ultrasound first
initialTests.action: For children and adolescents with suspected appendicitis and imaging need, use abdominal/right-lower-quadrant ultrasound as the initial imaging test when available and reliable.
initialTests.rationale: Ultrasound avoids ionizing radiation and is recommended as initial pediatric appendicitis imaging when clinical suspicion warrants imaging.

source.source_section: children and adolescents with suspected acute appendicitis: initial imaging
source.implementation_notes: Do not treat adult CT-first logic as the pediatric default.

initialTests.management_change: Positive ultrasound changes surgical consultation and appendicitis management; nondiagnostic ultrasound requires reassessment rather than false reassurance.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: IDSA gives a guideline recommendation rather than a single LR; ACEP summarizes pediatric ultrasound LR ranges in the clinical policy.
initialTests.limitations: Ultrasound performance depends on visualization, operator, body habitus, and local resources; nondiagnostic studies are not negative.

initialTests.label: Equivocal ultrasound appendicitis pathway
initialTests.action: If ultrasound is nondiagnostic or equivocal and suspicion remains, use surgical consultation, observation with serial examination, repeat ultrasound, MRI, or CT with IV contrast according to local resources and shared decision-making.
initialTests.rationale: Counting nondiagnostic ultrasound as negative can miss appendicitis; follow-up imaging or observation changes management when suspicion persists.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Persistent suspected pediatric appendicitis after equivocal or nondiagnostic ultrasound.

source.source_section: pediatric nondiagnostic ultrasound, observation, surgical consultation, repeat imaging, MRI/CT, and shared decision-making
source.implementation_notes: ACEP notes observation, consultation, hospital observation, serial ultrasound, MRI, or CT may be reasonable depending on resources and preferences.

initialTests.management_change: Changes need for observation, serial exam, surgical consult, repeat imaging, MRI/CT selection, and discharge restrictions.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: ACEP table reports weighted pediatric ultrasound LR+ about 14.4 and LR- about 0.23 across methods; nondiagnostic handling materially changes negative performance.
initialTests.limitations: LR values depend on study methods and whether nondiagnostic examinations are counted as negative, excluded, or managed with reassessment.

initialTests.label: Urinalysis with urine culture
initialTests.action: Obtain urine before antibiotics when UTI is possible, using age-appropriate collection; in high-risk or septic children, do not delay treatment while urine is obtained.
initialTests.rationale: UTI symptoms and abdominal/vomiting presentations overlap; urine testing changes antibiotics and follow-up.

source.source_section: urine collection, microscopy/culture, high-risk children, referral, and treatment principles
source.implementation_notes: Do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria; use local pediatric antimicrobial prescribing guidance.

initialTests.management_change: Positive urine testing changes antibiotics, pyelonephritis assessment, imaging/follow-up eligibility, and constipation/dysfunctional elimination counseling.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: This module does not assign urine dipstick LR by age; use NICE age-specific urine testing algorithms and local lab interpretation.
initialTests.limitations: Contamination and collection method matter; persistent symptoms after negative urine require reassessment.

initialTests.label: Pregnancy test for pregnancy-capable adolescent
initialTests.action: Obtain a pregnancy test for postmenarchal or pregnancy-capable adolescents with lower abdominal pain, pelvic pain, vomiting, syncope, bleeding, or pregnancy possibility.
initialTests.rationale: Pregnancy and ectopic pregnancy change imaging, differential, confidentiality, and emergency management.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Pregnancy, ectopic pregnancy, pregnancy of unknown location, or miscarriage complication.

source.source_section: pregnancy-related abdominal or pelvic pain and ectopic pregnancy symptoms/signs
source.implementation_notes: Use confidential adolescent care and local pregnancy-of-unknown-location pathway when positive.

initialTests.management_change: Positive pregnancy test changes ultrasound/beta-hCG pathway, medication/imaging choices, obstetric/gynecology consultation, and safety-netting.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Diagnostic test interpretation depends on assay and timing; this is a required applicability/safety test rather than a symptom LR item.
initialTests.limitations: Very early pregnancy can yield false-negative urine testing; persistent symptoms require reassessment and local protocol.

initialTests.label: Stool microbiology when indicated
initialTests.action: Order stool microbiology when there is blood or mucus in stool, suspected septicemia, immunocompromise, recent foreign travel, no improvement by day 7, diagnostic uncertainty, or outbreak/public-health concern.
initialTests.rationale: Routine stool testing is not needed for every gastroenteritis case, but targeted testing changes infection control and treatment.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Bacterial, parasitic, outbreak-related, or severe enteric infection.

source.source_section: stool microbiology indications and public health notification
source.implementation_notes: Public-health steps may be required for outbreaks; exact tests depend on local lab practice.

initialTests.management_change: Positive or indicated stool testing changes isolation, public health notification, antimicrobial decisions, follow-up, and return precautions.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached; testing is indication-driven.
initialTests.limitations: Negative stool testing does not exclude noninfectious abdominal pathology or early infection.

initialTests.label: Oral rehydration therapy threshold
initialTests.action: If clinical dehydration is present and shock is absent, give oral rehydration solution 50 mL/kg over 4 hours with small frequent amounts, continuing breastfeeding when applicable.
initialTests.rationale: Guideline-based ORS can prevent unnecessary IV fluids while ensuring children with clinical dehydration receive a concrete volume and reassessment plan.

source.source_section: oral rehydration for clinical dehydration
source.implementation_notes: Escalate to NG/IV route if ORS is refused, vomiting persists, or dehydration worsens.

initialTests.management_change: Clinical dehydration changes to weight-based ORS, observation, reassessment, and escalation if oral route fails.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to treatment threshold; management follows dehydration severity.
initialTests.limitations: Use IV/enteral fluids and urgent care for shock, severe dehydration, persistent vomiting, or inability to tolerate ORS.

initialTests.label: Avoid routine low-risk abdominal testing
initialTests.action: When the child is well-appearing, has no red flags, no focal pain, no dehydration, and a likely self-limited gastroenteritis pattern, avoid routine broad labs or imaging and provide reassessment/safety-netting.
initialTests.rationale: Over-testing can expose children to radiation, false positives, and unnecessary treatment; under-testing is unsafe when red flags are present.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Low-risk self-limited vomiting/diarrhea pattern after red flags are actively denied.

source.source_section: investigations guided by likely cause and most children not needing investigations
source.implementation_notes: This recommendation is only safe after documented red-flag, dehydration, urinary, pregnancy, and torsion screening.

initialTests.management_change: Changes from broad testing to safety-netted observation only when red flags are denied and follow-up is feasible.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: No LR is attached; this is a guideline-based stewardship rule with explicit limitations.
initialTests.limitations: Do not apply when pain is localized/progressive, vomiting is bilious, dehydration/shock exists, pregnancy/torsion is possible, fever is concerning, or follow-up is unreliable.

dispositionRules.label: Surgical or ED escalation
dispositionRules.action: Escalate to pediatric ED/surgery for bilious vomiting, peritonitis, progressive focal pain, obstruction concern, severe dehydration/shock, or persistent appendicitis concern.
dispositionRules.rationale: Surgical abdominal emergencies and severe dehydration need time-sensitive care that cannot be handled by generic outpatient advice.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Appendicitis complication, bowel obstruction, intussusception, perforation, incarcerated hernia, shock, or high-acuity abdominal illness.

source.source_section: acute abdominal pain management, red flags, transfer preparation, analgesia, IV access, and fluids
source.implementation_notes: Before transfer for possible surgical conditions, ensure analgesia, venous access, and fluids when clinically indicated.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes destination of care, transfer urgency, NPO/IV access, analgesia, fluids, imaging, and consult ownership.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to escalation disposition; bedside danger criteria drive action.
dispositionRules.limitations: Stable observation can be appropriate only when red flags are absent, reassessment is planned, and follow-up is reliable.

dispositionRules.label: Appendicitis imaging disposition
dispositionRules.action: For suspected pediatric appendicitis, use ultrasound-first imaging when needed; after nondiagnostic ultrasound, do not discharge solely on that result if clinical suspicion remains.
dispositionRules.rationale: Nondiagnostic ultrasound can miss appendicitis; persistent suspicion needs observation, surgical consultation, repeat ultrasound, MRI, or CT.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Suspected or incompletely excluded pediatric appendicitis.

source.source_section: pediatric ultrasound, nondiagnostic ultrasound, observation, consultation, MRI/CT, and shared decision-making
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric surgery/radiology resources and shared decision-making.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes from simple discharge to serial exam, observation, surgical consultation, repeat imaging, MRI, or CT with IV contrast when suspicion persists.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: ACEP reports weighted pediatric ultrasound LR+ about 14.4 and LR- about 0.23 across methods; nondiagnostic result handling changes negative reliability.
dispositionRules.limitations: Risk scores can support stratification but should not be used alone to decide no advanced imaging in pediatric suspected appendicitis.

dispositionRules.label: Gastroenteritis oral rehydration safety net
dispositionRules.action: If no red flags and oral intake is safe, continue usual feeds, encourage fluids, use ORS for increased-risk or dehydrated children, avoid fruit juices/carbonated drinks, and provide return precautions.
dispositionRules.rationale: A clinician-usable plan needs exact hydration actions and safety-net triggers rather than vague reassurance.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Likely gastroenteritis with low-risk or improving dehydration status.

source.source_section: fluid management, oral rehydration, post-rehydration care, and home advice
source.implementation_notes: Return precautions should include worsening pain, bilious vomiting, blood/mucus stool, poor intake, no urine, lethargy, or persistent vomiting.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes to ORS volume/frequency, feeding plan, avoidances, caregiver instructions, and reassessment timing.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to hydration disposition; severity and red flags drive management.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not use home plan for shock, altered mental status, bilious vomiting, peritonitis, unreliable follow-up, or failed oral rehydration.

dispositionRules.label: UTI testing treatment and follow-up route
dispositionRules.action: If UTI is suspected, obtain urine before antibiotics when feasible, treat according to upper versus lower UTI guidance, avoid treating asymptomatic bacteriuria, and address constipation or dysfunctional elimination after UTI.
dispositionRules.rationale: Pediatric UTI management changes with urinary symptoms, fever/systemic illness, culture results, and recurrence risk.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Lower UTI, upper UTI/pyelonephritis, recurrent UTI, or asymptomatic bacteriuria.

source.source_section: urine samples, antimicrobial prescribing links, asymptomatic bacteriuria, and preventing recurrence
source.implementation_notes: Use local antimicrobial guidance and imaging/follow-up criteria.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes antibiotics, culture follow-up, renal imaging eligibility, constipation/dysfunctional elimination management, and safety-netting.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned; urine testing and guideline criteria determine management.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not delay urgent antibiotics in high-risk or septic children solely to obtain urine.

dispositionRules.label: Adolescent pregnancy and pelvic emergency route
dispositionRules.action: For pregnancy-capable adolescents with lower abdominal/pelvic pain or pregnancy possibility, use confidential history, pregnancy testing, and ectopic/pregnancy-of-unknown-location escalation when warning symptoms are present.
dispositionRules.rationale: Pregnancy status materially changes imaging, medications, differential, confidentiality, and emergency disposition.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Pregnancy, ectopic pregnancy, miscarriage, pelvic emergency, or pregnancy of unknown location.

source.source_section: ectopic pregnancy symptoms and persistent symptoms requiring further investigation
source.implementation_notes: Use local adolescent confidentiality policy and obstetric/gynecology escalation pathway.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes pregnancy testing, ultrasound/beta-hCG pathway, medication/imaging constraints, gynecology consultation, and discharge instructions.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; pregnancy testing and warning symptoms change action.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not rely on denial of sexual activity alone when pregnancy is biologically possible and symptoms are concerning.

criteria_options.label: Vomiting metabolic screen: check glucose/ketones when BGL <3 mmol/L or >11 mmol/L; treat hypoglycemia when BGL <2.6 mmol/L
criteria_options.criteria_text: In a vomiting child, consider blood glucose and ketones if BGL is <3 mmol/L or >11 mmol/L; treat as hypoglycemia if BGL is <2.6 mmol/L.

criteria_options.label: Vomiting shock resuscitation: sodium chloride 0.9% bolus 10-20 mL/kg
criteria_options.criteria_text: If shock is present in a vomiting child, treat with a 10-20 mL/kg bolus of sodium chloride 0.9% while evaluating the cause.

criteria_options.label: Ondansetron support if age >6 months: 8-15 kg 2 mg, 15-30 kg 4 mg, >30 kg 8 mg
criteria_options.criteria_text: Ondansetron may support oral hydration in children >6 months; suggested initial dose is 2 mg for 8-15 kg, 4 mg for 15-30 kg, and 8 mg for >30 kg.
